summary: The q6 dataset shows uniform activity across all ten problems (q1-q10), each reporting exactly 100 issues with no proof completion or status differentiation available. The flat distribution and absence of granular metadata (issue types, dates, severity) limit confident prioritization, suggesting the dataset may be a placeholder, aggregate cap, or in need of richer instrumentation before actionable triage is possible.
problems: All problems (q1-q10) report an identical count of 100 issues, which strongly suggests a data ceiling, truncation, or default-value artifact rather than genuine per-problem activity
No proof status data is populated (none marked solved, in-progress, or stalled), making it impossible to identify which problems are near completion
No issue-type breakdown (e.g., logical gaps, notation, missing lemmas) is provided, preventing pattern analysis of common failure modes
No temporal or traction signals (recent activity, contributor counts) exist to distinguish high-momentum problems from dormant ones
highlights: Complete coverage: every problem in the dataset has tracked issues, so no problem is being ignored
Consistent data structure across all ten problems enables straightforward comparative tooling once richer fields are added
The uniform 100-issue count provides a clean baseline against which future differentiated metrics can be measured

suggested_todos.title: Investigate whether the uniform 100-issue count is a real value or a truncation/cap artifact, and fix the data pipeline if so
Add proof-status fields (solved / in-progress / blocked) per problem to enable solvability assessment
Enrich issues with type, severity, and timestamp metadata to support pattern and traction analysis
Once enriched data is available, re-run triage to identify the top 2-3 near-solvable problems for the week
Define and document a standard issue taxonomy for the math research team to ensure consistent future reporting
